1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
98	  \[
99	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
100	      \longrightarrow 0
101	  \]
102	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
103	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
104	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
105	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
106	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
107	  \[
108	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
109	  \]
110	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
111	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
112	  \[
113	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
114	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
115	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
116	  \]
117	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
118	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
119	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
120	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
124	
125	\begin{definition}[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
126	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
127	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
128	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
129	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
130	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
131	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
132	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
133	  % L5594--5617).
134	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
135	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
136	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
137	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
138	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
139	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
140	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
141	  % between abelian categories and assume
142	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
143	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
144	  % \begin{enumerate}
145	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
146	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
147	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
148	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
149	  % \end{enumerate}"
150	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
151	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
152	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
153	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
154	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
155	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
156	  \[
157	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
158	  \]
159	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
160	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
161	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
162	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
163	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
164	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
165	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
166	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
167	
168	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
169	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
170	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
171	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
172	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
176	
177	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
178	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
179	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
180	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
181	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
182	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
183	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
184	lift.
185	
186	\begin{lemma}[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
187	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
188	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
189	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
190	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
191	  \[
192	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
193	  \]
194	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
195	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
196	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
197	  \[
198	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
199	  \]
200	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
201	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
202	  \[
203	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
204	      \longrightarrow 0
205	  \]
206	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
207	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
208	\end{lemma}
209	
210	\begin{proof}
211	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
212	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
213	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
214	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
215	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
216	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
217	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
218	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
219	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
220	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
221	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
222	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
223	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
224	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
225	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
226	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
227	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
228	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
229	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
230	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
231	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
232	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
233	\end{proof}
234	
235	\section{The dimension-shift step}
236	
237	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
238	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
239	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
240	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
241	complex-level homology long exact sequence
242	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
243	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
244	sub-lemma below.
245	
246	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
247	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
248	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
249	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
250	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
251	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
252	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
253	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
254	  % between abelian categories and assume
255	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
256	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
257	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
258	  % \begin{enumerate}
259	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
260	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
261	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
262	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
263	  % \end{enumerate}
264	  % In each of the three cases
265	  % $$
266	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
267	  % $$
268	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
269	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
270	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
271	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
272	  \[
273	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
274	  \]
275	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
276	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
277	  \begin{enumerate}
278	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
279	      furnish isomorphisms
280	      \[
281	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
282	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
283	      \]
284	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
285	      \[
286	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
287	      \]
288	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
289	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
290	  \end{enumerate}
291	\end{lemma}
292	
293	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
294	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
295	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
296	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
297	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
298	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
299	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
300	\begin{proof}
301	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
302	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
303	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
304	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
305	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
306	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
307	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
308	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
309	
310	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
311	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
312	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
313	  complexes
314	  \[
315	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
316	  \]
317	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
318	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
319	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
320	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
321	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
322	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
323	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
324	  \[
325	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
326	      \longrightarrow 0
327	  \]
328	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
329	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
330	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
331	  \[
332	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
333	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
334	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
335	  \]
336	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
337	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
338	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
339	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
340	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
341	  sequence of right-derived functors
342	  \[
343	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
344	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
345	  \]
346	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
347	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
348	  \[
349	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
350	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
351	  \]
352	
353	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
354	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
355	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
356	  \[
357	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
358	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
359	  \]
360	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
361	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
362	  In the lowest degree the segment
363	  \[
364	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
365	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
366	  \]
367	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
368	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
369	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
370	  part (2).
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
374	
375	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
376	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
377	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
378	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
379	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
380	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
381	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
382	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
383	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
384	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
385	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
386	  % L5785--5811).
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
388	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
389	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
390	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
391	  % $$
392	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
393	  % $$
394	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
395	  % $RF$."
396	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
397	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
398	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
399	  % and we obtain an exact functor
400	  % $$
401	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
402	  % $$
403	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
404	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
405	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
406	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
407	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
408	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
409	  Let
410	  \[
411	    0 \longrightarrow A
412	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
413	      \longrightarrow \cdots
414	  \]
415	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
416	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
417	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
418	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
419	  \[
420	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
421	  \]
422	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
423	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
424	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
425	  object.
426	\end{theorem}
427	
428	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
429	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
430	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
431	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
432	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
433	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
434	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
435	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
436	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
437	% the long exact cohomology sequence
438	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
439	% to an exact sequence
440	% $$
441	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
442	% $$
443	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
444	\begin{proof}
445	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
446	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
447	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
448	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
449	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
450	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
451	  resolution into the
452	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
453	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
454	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
455	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
456	  \[
457	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
458	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
459	  \]
460	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
461	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
462	
463	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
464	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
465	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
466	  \[
467	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
468	      = (R^0 G)(A),
469	  \]
470	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
471	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
472	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
473	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
474	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
475	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
476	  \[
477	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
478	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
479	  \]
480	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
481	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
482	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
483	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
484	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
485	
486	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
487	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
488	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
489	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
490	  \[
491	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
492	  \]
493	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
494	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
495	  isomorphisms down the staircase
496	  \[
497	    (R^n G)(A)
498	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
499	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
500	      \;\cong\; \cdots
501	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
502	  \]
503	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
504	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
505	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
506	  \[
507	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
508	  \]
509	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
510	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
511	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
512	  \[
513	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
514	      \;\cong\;
515	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
516	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
517	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
518	  \]
519	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
520	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
521	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
522	
523	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
524	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
525	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
526	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
527	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
528	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
529	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
530	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
531	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
532	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
533	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
534	\end{proof}
535

113	
114	# Mathlib Analogist
115	
116	You are the read-only Mathlib-analogist subagent with **two modes**: API alignment (the default) and cross-domain inspiration. The directive's `## Mode` line selects which.
117	
118	## Mode: api-alignment
119	
120	The plan agent points you at a project declaration, a proposed definition, or a design question, and asks "what would Mathlib do, and is the project doing that?". Your job:
121	
122	1. Identify the actual design decisions at play (often multiple compressed into one question).
123	2. Find how Mathlib has resolved analogous decisions.
124	3. Compare the project's path to Mathlib's idiom.
125	4. Report whether the project should align with the idiom — and, if not, what the cost of the divergence is.
126	
127	You do **not** rubber-stamp the project's choice. If Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project skipped it (most often because the prerequisite infrastructure looked hard), say so plainly. "It works" is not enough — the cost of API fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and code that can't compose with Mathlib is real.
128	
129	## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
130	
131	The plan agent points you at a *structural problem* — a categorical, topological, algebraic, or analytic shape that's been resisting the project's current approach — and asks "has Mathlib solved this same shape somewhere else, possibly in a completely different mathematical field?". Your job:
132	
133	1. Distill the structural problem to its categorical / algebraic / topological core, stripping away the project's specific objects.
134	2. Search *broadly* across Mathlib for that shape. Cross domains aggressively — a sheaf condition on schemes shares structure with a sheaf condition on topological spaces; a descent argument in alg geom shares structure with a descent argument in functional analysis; a fixed-point setup in measure theory shares structure with order-theoretic Knaster–Tarski.
135	3. For each analogue you find, extract the *technique* used there (the tactic combination, the categorical lemma, the unbundling trick, the inductive step).
136	4. Map the technique back to the project's setting. State concretely what porting the technique would look like.
137	
138	You are explicitly NOT checking API alignment in this mode. The goal is to surface unfamiliar territory that may be borrowable, not to validate the project's existing definitions. Distant analogues are MORE valuable than nearby ones — the planner already knows the nearby ones.
139	
140	## Scope
141	
142	**One design question per invocation.** A design question may be broad ("how should we represent the Picard scheme?") or narrow ("instance-based vs. predicate-based"). Multiple Mathlib precedents in the SAME question are welcome. What you should NOT do is sprawl across unrelated decisions in one call.
143	
144	**Read-only.** You may read:
145	
146	- Project files (`.lean`, blueprint chapters, `references/`).
147	- Mathlib (via `archon-lean-lsp` MCP: `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, hover, signature lookup).
148	- Any existing `analogies/<slug>.md` summaries from prior calls.
149	
150	You may write:
151	
152	- `analogies/<slug>.md` — persistent design-rationale file future iters re-read.
153	- `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path) — your report.
154	
155	You may **NOT** modify project source, blueprint, or any state file.
156	
157	## Directive Format
158	
159	The directive begins with `## Mode: api-alignment` or `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration`. The remaining fields differ by mode.
160	
161	### Mode: api-alignment
162	
163	```markdown
164	# Mathlib Analogist Directive
165	
166	## Mode
167	api-alignment
168	
169	## Slug
170	<slug>
171	
172	## Design question
173	<the question. One question per directive.>
174	
175	## Project artifact(s) under question
176	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
177	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
178	
179	## Why now
180	<one or two sentences: what the dispatching agent is about to do (write, refactor, decide), and why a Mathlib precedent would inform it.>
181	
182	## Hints (optional)
183	<Mathlib namespaces, related concepts, or specific declarations the dispatcher suspects are relevant.>
184	
185	## Severity expectation
186	<one of: routine | high-stakes>
187	- routine: cheap sanity check on a small choice
188	- high-stakes: this design will be load-bearing; be strict about idiom adherence
189	```
190	
191	### Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
192	
193	```markdown
194	# Mathlib Analogist Directive
195	
196	## Mode
197	cross-domain-inspiration
198	
199	## Slug
200	<slug>
201	
202	## Structural problem
203	<the categorical / algebraic / topological shape, stated as abstractly
204	as possible. Strip away the project's specific objects — the goal is
205	to make the shape recognizable across domains. Example: "we need a
206	sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of modules over
207	a site whose covering family is finite flat" rather than "we need a
208	sheaf condition for quasi-coherent modules on Spec(O_K)".>
209	
210	## Failed approaches
211	<one short bullet per attempt that didn't work. Format:
212	- <approach>: <underlying obstacle that stopped it>.
213	- ...
214	
215	The goal is to tell me what NOT to suggest porting back — if the
216	analogue's technique boils down to "do exactly what failed", skip it.>
217	
218	## Search radius
219	<one of: wide | narrow>
220	- wide: search any Mathlib domain — functional analysis, probability,
221	  combinatorics, order theory, anything. Preferred for genuinely
222	  novel structural shapes.
223	- narrow: stay within the project's general area (e.g. algebraic
224	  geometry / commutative algebra / category theory) but cross
225	  sub-areas. Preferred when the planner suspects the analogue is
226	  "one shelf over" rather than across the library.
227	
228	## Hints (optional)
229	<Mathlib namespaces, technique names, or specific declarations the
230	dispatcher suspects share structure. Use sparingly — your value is
231	in finding analogues the dispatcher couldn't.>
232	```
233	
234	## Workflow
235	
236	1. **Read the directive completely.** Note the `## Mode` line first — your workflow branches on it.
237	
238	### api-alignment mode
239	
240	2. **Read the project artifact(s)** and their surrounding context — imports, neighboring declarations, the blueprint chapter the declaration corresponds to (per the `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). The blueprint often makes the mathematical intent explicit where the Lean only hints at it.
241	
242	3. **Identify the open design decision(s).** A directive that says "should this be a typeclass?" usually compresses several decisions (bundled vs unbundled, predicate vs structure, instance vs explicit). Name each.
243	
244	4. **Locate Mathlib precedents.** Use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` on the relevant names and types. Read 2–5 Mathlib files that resolve analogous decisions. For each, note:
245	   - What Mathlib chose (typeclass / structure / predicate / function).
246	   - Why (legible from the docstring, naming convention, or how the choice composes with surrounding API).
247	   - How the project's current or proposed approach compares.
248	
249	5. **Compare and judge.** For each open decision, write:
250	   - The Mathlib idiom (with citation: `Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
251	   - The project's path (or proposed path).
252	   - The gap: identical, divergent-but-equivalent, divergent-with-cost, divergent-and-wrong.
253	   - For "divergent-with-cost" and "divergent-and-wrong": the concrete cost (bridge lemmas needed, downstream files that can't consume the project's API, parallel infrastructure that will eventually need to be unified).
254	
255	6. **Render a verdict per decision.** One of:
256	   - **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — the project should use the idiom; if shipped code diverges, refactor.
257	   - **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY** — divergence is the right call for this project; document why in `analogies/<slug>.md`.
258	   - **PROCEED** — no Mathlib precedent applies; project's path is reasonable.
259	   - **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** — Mathlib doesn't have the idiom yet; the project must build it (this is the case for genuinely new infrastructure).
260	
261	7. **Write the persistent analogy file** to `analogies/<slug>.md`. Format below.
262	
263	8. **Write the report** to `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md`.
264	
265	### cross-domain-inspiration mode
266	
267	2. **Re-state the structural problem in your own words** as the most abstract version you can defend. Strip the project's objects. Aim for a one-paragraph re-statement that names only the categorical / topological / algebraic primitives.
268	
269	3. **Brainstorm analogues across Mathlib.** Before searching, list 5–10 mathematical situations *in other Mathlib domains* that share the same structural shape. Be aggressive about distance — if the problem is sheaf-shaped, look at probability sheaves AND topological sheaves AND order-theoretic sheaves. If it's a fixed-point setup, look at Knaster–Tarski (order theory), Banach (functional analysis), Schauder (topology), Kleene (lattice theory), and so on.
270	
271	4. **Verify analogues with the LSP.** For each candidate, use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` to confirm the construction actually lives in Mathlib. Read the Mathlib file. Discard candidates that turn out to be only superficially similar.
272	
273	5. **Extract the technique.** For each verified analogue, write:
274	   - The Mathlib citation (`Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
275	   - The structural problem solved there, in the same abstract vocabulary you used in step 2.
276	   - The technique: the tactic combination, the key lemma, the categorical reformulation, the unbundling step — whatever the actual proof or construction depends on.
277	   - The mapping back: how would this technique translate to the project's setting? Be concrete — name the project's objects, point to the corresponding Mathlib constructs.
278	   - The cost of porting: typeclass scaffolding needed, lemmas to be ported, anything the Mathlib version assumes that the project doesn't have.
279	
280	6. **Skip analogues whose technique matches a "Failed approaches" bullet.** If the analogue's technique reduces to something the directive already tried, drop it.
281	
282	7. **Render a verdict per analogue.** One of:
283	   - **ANALOGUE_FOUND** — a Mathlib precedent with a portable technique. The planner should consider trying it next iter.
284	   - **PARTIAL_ANALOGUE** — Mathlib solves a related but not identical shape; the technique is suggestive but not directly portable. Worth a deeper look only if the higher-priority options fail.
285	   - **NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE** — Mathlib has the shape but the technique doesn't generalize beyond Mathlib's specific setting.
286	
287	8. **Rank.** Order ANALOGUE_FOUND verdicts by porting cost (lowest first). The planner reads top-to-bottom.
288	
289	9. **Write the persistent file** to `analogies/<slug>.md` and the report to `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md`. Format below — the cross-domain section is separate from the api-alignment section.
290	
291	## Persistent file format (`analogies/<slug>.md`)
292	
293	The persistent file's body depends on the mode.
294	
295	### api-alignment
296	
297	```markdown

299	
300	## Mode
301	api-alignment
302

304	<slug>
305

308

311

313	- <file>:<lines> — <one-line summary>
314	- ...
315	
316	## Decisions identified
317	
318	For each open decision, one block:
319	
320	### Decision: <name>
321	
322	- **Mathlib idiom**: <precedent>. Cite: `Mathlib.X.Y.Foo` (path:line). Why Mathlib chose it: <one paragraph>.
323	- **Project's current path**: <what the project does or proposes>.
324	- **Gap**: identical | divergent-equivalent | divergent-with-cost | divergent-and-wrong.
325	- **Cost of divergence (if any)**: <bridge lemmas, fragmented API, downstream blockage>.
326	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY | PROCEED | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
327	
328	## Recommendation

330	<one paragraph: what the project should do given the verdicts above. If ALIGN, what the refactor should look like.>
331	```
332	
333	### cross-domain-inspiration
334	
335	```markdown
336	# Analogy: <structural problem>
337	
338	## Mode
339	cross-domain-inspiration
340	
341	## Slug
342	<slug>
343	
344	## Iteration
345	<NNN>
346	
347	## Structural problem (abstracted)
348	<the structural shape, in domain-neutral language. Two or three sentences.>
349	
350	## Failed approaches (from directive)
351	- <approach>: <obstacle>.
352	- ...
353	
354	## Analogues found
355	
356	For each analogue, ranked by porting cost (lowest first):
357	
358	### Analogue: <Mathlib citation, e.g. `Mathlib.Order.FixedPoints.lfp`>
359	
360	- **Domain**: <order theory | functional analysis | probability | category theory | ...>.
361	- **Same structural problem there**: <re-state the Mathlib problem in the same abstract vocabulary used for the project's problem>.
362	- **Technique**: <the proof technique, key lemma, tactic combination, or construction step that solves it. Be specific.>
363	- **Mapping to project**: <one paragraph. Name the project's objects and the corresponding Mathlib constructs.>
364	- **Porting cost**: <low | medium | high>. <one sentence on what would need to be built.>
365	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND | PARTIAL_ANALOGUE | NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE.
366

370	```
371	
372	## Report format
373	
374	The report's body depends on the mode.
375	
376	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Mode`, `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, plus the mode-specific question/problem restatement, the verdicts/analogues table, and the overall verdict. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty** — do NOT pad sections with "(none)" / "N/A" content.
377	
378	In api-alignment mode:
379	
380	- `## Must-fix-this-iter`: omit when no ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB verdict applies to shipped code.
381	- `## Major`: omit when no ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB verdict applies to in-proposal code.
382	- `## Informational`: omit when there's nothing worth surfacing beyond what's already in the verdicts table.
383	
384	In cross-domain-inspiration mode:
385	
386	- `## Top suggestion`: required only when at least one ANALOGUE_FOUND verdict exists. When every analogue was NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE, replace with a one-sentence "no analogues with portable techniques surfaced" line under the verdicts table; do not invent a top suggestion to fill the slot.
387	- `## Discarded`: omit when no analogues were considered-and-rejected.
388	
389	In both modes: stop searching once you have a confident finding. Don't enumerate near-misses exhaustively to look thorough — three solid analogues outrank a list of fifteen with one-line "doesn't quite work" notes.

1	---
2	name: effort-breaker
3	description: Split ONE high-effort blueprint declaration into a \uses-linked chain of smaller, easier sub-lemmas — each with its own statement, informal proof, and \uses — so the prover formalizes small pieces instead of one large goal. On-demand; iterative (re-dispatch to break a still-hard sub-lemma further, down to sentence level).
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against ONE target declaration that is too big to formalize in
10	    one go — a high-`effort_local` node, or one the progress-critic flags as a
11	    repeated blocker. I am most valuable on a **frontier** node (deps already
12	    done): it is the live bottleneck, and decomposing it exposes small ready
13	    pieces next iter.
14	  - Name the target label in `## Target`, and say **how aggressively to break**
15	    in `## Granularity`: "one level" (the proof's main steps) by default, or
16	    "fine — one mathematical claim per lemma" when a prior, coarser break still
17	    left a hard piece. Decomposition is ITERATIVE and has no floor: if a break
18	    doesn't drop the difficulty enough, re-dispatch me on the still-hard
19	    sub-lemma asking me to break it further (sentence-by-sentence, like the
20	    fine-grained prover). Do not pre-empt this — just break, see if it helped,
21	    break more if not.
22	  - Give me the proof sketch / reference structure to cut along in
23	    `## Proof structure` so I split at real mathematical seams, not arbitrary
24	    points.
25	  - Do NOT dispatch me at an already-done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) — there
26	    is nothing to break. Do NOT dispatch me at an ∞-effort node (no informal
27	    proof to decompose) — send a dag-walker / blueprint-writer to write the
28	    proof first, then me to break it.
29	  - **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** (sync_leanok's job). `\mathlibok` only
30	    on genuine Mathlib anchors.
31	---
32	
33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.
38	
39	## Directive Format
40	
41	```markdown
42	# Effort Breaker Directive
43	
44	## Slug
45	<slug>
46	
47	## Target
48	<the blueprint label to break, e.g. thm:flattening_stratification_exists>
49	
50	## Granularity
51	<"one level" (the proof's main steps) | "fine — one mathematical claim per
52	 lemma" (when a coarser break already happened and a piece is still hard)>
53	
54	## Proof structure
55	<the seams to cut along: the steps of the target's informal proof, or the
56	 structure of the source proof. The dispatcher gives you this so you split at
57	 real mathematical boundaries.>
58	
59	## Strategy context
60	<the slice of STRATEGY.md that matters — what this result supports. You do NOT
61	 read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
62	
63	## References
64	- <path/to/reference.md>: <which proof / steps live here>
65	```
66	
67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.
78	
79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.
80	
81	## Markers and citation (same rules as every blueprint agent)
82	
83	- **Never add `\leanok`** — it is the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase's job.
84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.
95	
96	## Workflow
97	
98	1. Read the directive and the target block on disk.
99	2. Read the references the directive names; identify the proof's steps.
100	3. Author the sub-lemma chain (statement + proof + `\uses` for each), bottom-up so later lemmas can `\uses{}` earlier ones.
101	4. Rewrite the target's proof to invoke the chain.
102	5. For any step needing a source you lack, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
103	6. Re-query the graph; confirm the target's effort dropped and no `\uses{}` broke.
104	7. Write your report.
105	
106	## Logging
107	
108	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/effort-breaker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
109	
110	```markdown
111	# Effort Breaker Report
112	
113	## Slug
114	<slug>
115	
116	## Target
117	<label>
118	
119	## Status
120	<COMPLETE — target re-expressed as a chain | PARTIAL — a step could not be broken/proved (listed below)>
121	
122	## Effort before → after
123	- target `effort_local`: <before> → <after>
124	- sub-lemmas added: <count>
125	
126	## Chain added (target ← L_n ← … ← L1)
127	- `\label{lem:L1}` `\lean{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <statement, one line> (effort ≈ <small>)
128	- `\label{lem:L2}` … — <statement> (\uses{lem:L1})
129	- ...
130	- Target `<label>` proof rewritten: `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}`.
131	
132	## Still hard (re-break candidates)
133	- `lem:Lk` — still large; re-dispatch the breaker at finer granularity, OR <why it's irreducible>.
134	
135	## Could not decompose (strategy items)
136	- <step> — <why: genuinely atomic / novel / statement looks wrong>.
137	
138	## References consulted
139	- `references/<file>.md` — <verbatim content taken>.
140	
141	## Notes for dispatcher
142	- `\lean{}` names I assigned by convention (confirm/scaffold): <list>.
143	- needed macros / cross-chapter concerns.
144	```
145	
146	## Return Value
147	
148	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

471	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
472	
473	\begin{lemma}
474	
475	\leanok
476	[Standard-cover {\v C}ech-complex vanishing on affines]
477	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
479	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
480	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
481	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
482	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52).
483	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
484	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
485	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
486	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
487	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
488	  % all $p > 0$."
489	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
490	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
491	  vanishing).}
492	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
493	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
494	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
495	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
496	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
497	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} has vanishing cohomology in all positive
498	  degrees:
499	  \[
500	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
501	  \]
502	  Equivalently, the extended complex
503	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}}
504	  \to \cdots\) of localisations is exact. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech
505	  vanishing only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing
506	  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the separate
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
508	  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
509	\end{lemma}
510	
511	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
512	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
513	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
514	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
515	% ...
516	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
517	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
518	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
519	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
520	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
521	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
522	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
523	% Let us define a homotopy
524	% $$
525	% h :
526	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
527	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
528	% \longrightarrow
529	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
530	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
531	% $$
532	% by the rule
533	% $$
534	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
535	% $$
536	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
537	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
538	\begin{proof}
539	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
540	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
541	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
542	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
543	  \[
544	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
545	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
546	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
547	  \]
548	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
549	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
550	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}).
551	  Exactness may be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime
552	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\) (Algebra, Lemma
553	  \texttt{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}). Choose an index
554	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
555	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
556	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
557	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
558	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
559	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
560	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
561	  \(p > 0\).
562	\end{proof}
563	
564	\begin{lemma}
565	[Serre vanishing on affines]
566	  \label{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}
567	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_serre_vanishing}
568	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on
571	  % affines) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L145--155).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
573	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
574	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
575	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
576	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
577	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
578	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
579	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
580	  affines).}
581	  Let \(U\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
582	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Then the sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) vanishes in
583	  all positive degrees:
584	  \[
585	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
586	  \]
587	\end{lemma}
588	
589	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
590	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
591	% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
592	% the affine opens of $X$.
593	% As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
594	% open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
595	% Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
596	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
597	% hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
598	% another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
599	% The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
600	% of $\mathcal{B}$, see
601	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
602	% Hence (2) holds.
603	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
604	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
605	\begin{proof}
606	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
607	  Take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis \(\mathcal{B}\) of the topology and the
608	  standard open covers of affine opens as the admissible covers. The intersection of
609	  two standard opens in an affine is again a standard open, so \(\mathcal{B}\) is
610	  closed under the relevant intersections; and the standard covers form a cofinal
611	  system among the open covers of any basic open. The standard-cover {\v C}ech
612	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} supplies the acyclicity
613	  hypothesis. The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
614	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) then identifies the sheaf cohomology
615	  of \(\mathcal{F}\) on each basic open with its {\v C}ech cohomology for a standard
616	  cover, whence \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
617	\end{proof}
618	
619	\begin{lemma}
620	[{\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis]
621	  \label{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis}
623	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
624	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
625	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (a variant of \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish}; the
626	  % basis-comparison criterion for {\v C}ech vs.\ sheaf cohomology) (read from
627	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1714). The application context (its use
628	  % in the proof of \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}) is quoted
629	  % separately as SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below, read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
630	  % L157--173.

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
p4-derived-les

## Design question
To build the abstract "an acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor"
theorem (Leray's acyclicity, Stacks Tag 015E) at the level of Mathlib's
`CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerived n` objects, the kernel step is a **dimension
shift across a short exact sequence**: for an additive left-exact `G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ`
(𝒜 abelian with enough injectives) and a short exact sequence `0 → A → J → Z → 0`
with `J` right-`G`-acyclic, produce the connecting isomorphisms
`(R^k G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+1} G)(A)` (k ≥ 1) and `(R^1 G)(A) ≅ coker(G J → G Z)`.

This requires a **long exact sequence of right-derived functors** associated to a
SES. The plan's current route builds it by (a) a horseshoe lift of the SES to a
degreewise-split SES of injective resolutions
(`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`, which the plan agent has VERIFIED is ABSENT
from Mathlib — a project-side build), then (b) applying `G` degreewise (split ⇒
preserved), then (c) the complex-level homology LES
(`ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁/₂/₃` + `δ`, VERIFIED present), then (d)
transporting along `InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj` (VERIFIED present).

**The question:** Is the explicit horseshoe (`ofShortExact`) the cheapest Mathlib-aligned
path, or does Mathlib already provide the SES → LES of derived functors by another
mechanism that avoids building a horseshoe — e.g.
- a derived-category / triangulated route (`DerivedCategory 𝒜`, distinguished triangle
  of a SES, cohomology LES of a triangle, reconciled with `Functor.rightDerived` via
  `isoRightDerivedObj` or an `Ext`-level bridge), or
- a snake-lemma argument on a SINGLE chosen injective resolution + its cokernel complex
  (avoiding the full horseshoe), or
- any existing `Abelian.Ext` / `DerivedFunctor` LES that specializes to `rightDerived`?

Tell me the cleanest **Mathlib-aligned** construction of this dimension-shift / LES,
and whether the horseshoe is genuinely the right project-side gap to fill or an
avoidable detour.

## Project artifact(s) under question
- `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex` — the whole chapter; in
  particular `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` (horseshoe), `lem:acyclic_dimension_shift`
  (the kernel), `lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived` (Leray 015E).
- Target Lean names (to be scaffolded into `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean`):
  `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic`, `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`,
  `Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic`, `Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`.

## Why now
The plan agent is about to scaffold `AcyclicResolution.lean` and dispatch a
`mathlib-build` prover at it THIS iter. The scaffold shape (do we scaffold an explicit
`ofShortExact` horseshoe, or a leaner snake/derived-category construction?) depends on
your answer. This is the hardest remaining phase (P4); the horseshoe is its dominant
Lean-feasibility risk. Getting the route right before scaffolding saves prover iters.

## Hints
- Verified present: `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj`,
  `Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf`, `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`
  (all `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/RightDerived.lean`);
  `ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁/₂/₃`, `ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ`
  (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean`).
- Verified ABSENT (grep over `Mathlib/`): any `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`,
  any horseshoe, any `shortExact`/`longExact`/`δ` inside
  `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/RightDerived.lean`.
- Look at: `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Derived/` (if present), `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/`
  (`DerivedCategory`, `HomologySequence`, `Ext`), `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/Ext`.

## Severity expectation
high-stakes

15	the kernel step is a dimension shift across a SES `0 → A → J → Z → 0` (J right-G-
16	acyclic) producing `(R^k G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+1} G)(A)` (k ≥ 1) and
17	`(R^1 G)(A) ≅ coker(G J → G Z)`. This needs a SES → LES of right-derived functors.
18	Is the explicit horseshoe (`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`) the cheapest Mathlib-
19	aligned path, or does Mathlib already provide SES → LES of derived functors another
20	way (derived-category triangle, snake on a single resolution, or an Ext-level LES
21	that specializes to `rightDerived`)?
22	
23	## Project artifact(s)
24	- `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex` — whole chapter;
25	  `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` (horseshoe), `lem:acyclic_dimension_shift`
26	  (kernel), `lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived` (Leray 015E).
27	- Target Lean: `Functor.IsRightAcyclic`, `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`,
28	  `Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic`, `Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`.
29	
30	## Decisions identified
31	
32	### Decision: how to obtain the SES → LES of `Functor.rightDerived n G`
33	
34	- **Mathlib idiom**: There is **no** SES → LES for `Functor.rightDerived n`. The
35	  closest analogues are NOT applicable:
36	  - *Ext LES* (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/ExactSequences.lean`,
37	    `covariantSequence` / `contravariantSequence`, `covariant_sequence_exact₁/₂/₃`).
38	    These are built from the cohomological functors `preadditiveCoyoneda` /
39	    `preadditiveYoneda` applied to `triangleOfSES`'s homology sequence. They derive
40	    the LES of `Ext^n(X, –)` / `Ext^n(–, Y)` — i.e. of **derived Hom** — NOT of a
41	    general additive functor `G`. The project's `G = f_*` is not a Hom-functor, so
42	    this LES cannot be specialized to it. (`Ext.lean` defines `Ext` as
43	    `Abelian.Ext`, derived from `Hom` in the derived category — orthogonal to a
44	    user-supplied `G`.)
45	  - *Derived-category triangulated route* (`triangleOfSES` +
46	    `triangleOfSES_distinguished` in
47	    `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/ShortExact.lean`, plus
48	    `Pretriangulated.homologySequence_exact₁/₂/₃`). This gives the LES for a
49	    triangulated functor between derived categories. To feed `Functor.rightDerived n
50	    G` into it one needs the bridge
51	    `F.rightDerived n ≅ Hⁿ ∘ F.rightDerivedFunctorPlus ∘ Q` with
52	    `F.rightDerivedFunctorPlus : DerivedCategory.Plus C ⥤ DerivedCategory.Plus D`.
53	    **That bridge does not exist** — it is an explicit TODO in the module docstring
54	    of `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/RightDerived.lean` (lines 40–47):
55	    "refactor `Functor.rightDerived` … when the necessary material enters mathlib:
56	    derived categories, injective/projective derivability structures, existence of
57	    derived functors from derivability structures. Eventually … redefined using
58	    `F.rightDerivedFunctorPlus`." Building it requires the injective derivability
59	    structure + existence-of-derived-functor machinery (the
60	    `CategoryTheory/Localization/DerivabilityStructure/*` + `Functor/Derived/*`
61	    framework) AND a comparison theorem — far more than the horseshoe.
62	- **Project's current path**: build `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` (dual
63	  Horseshoe Lemma) → apply G degreewise (split ⇒ preserved) → complex-level homology
64	  LES (`ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ/homology_exact₁/₂/₃`, present in
65	  `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean:282–314`) → transport along
66	  `InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj` (present).
67	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL. The LES is genuinely absent for general `G`; the
68	  horseshoe is one self-contained way to manufacture it. The two "shortcut"
69	  alternatives are each strictly more expensive (Ext: wrong generality, unusable;
70	  derived-category: an explicitly-deferred Mathlib TODO).
71	- **Cost of divergence (if any)**: none — the horseshoe is the *cheaper* gap. The
72	  derived-category route would force the project to build (or wait for) the entire
73	  `rightDerivedFunctorPlus` bridge.
74	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (horseshoe is the right gap).
75	
76	### Decision: shape of `IsRightAcyclic`
77	
78	- **Mathlib idiom**: vanishing predicates over an object are `class … : Prop`
79	  (cf. `Injective`, `Projective` in
80	  `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Preadditive/Injective/Basic.lean`). The companion
81	  vanishing fact is `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` which states
82	  `IsZero ((F.rightDerived (n+1)).obj J)` for injective `J` — i.e. the
83	  index-shifted form `∀ k, IsZero ((R^{k+1} G) J)`.
84	- **Project's current path**: blueprint `def:right_acyclic` adopts exactly the
85	  index-shifted vanishing `(R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0 ∀ k`.
86	- **Gap**: identical (good). A `class IsRightAcyclic (G) (J) : Prop` wrapping
87	  `∀ k, IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k+1)).obj J)` mirrors Mathlib, and an `instance`
88	  deriving it for injectives from `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` is free.
89	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (align field shape with the `isZero …_succ` quantifier).
90	
91	## Recommendation
92	
93	Scaffold the **horseshoe** (`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`) — it is the right,
94	necessary, self-contained project-side gap, not a detour. Do NOT scaffold a derived-
95	category route: its prerequisite (`rightDerivedFunctorPlus` + injective derivability
96	structure) is an open Mathlib TODO and dwarfs the horseshoe. Do NOT try to specialize
97	the Ext LES: it is derived-Hom-specific and cannot see a general `G = f_*`.
98	
99	Concrete scaffold shape for the horseshoe and downstream:
100	1. `IsRightAcyclic G J : Prop` as a `class` = `∀ k, IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k+1)).obj J)`;
101	   instance for `[Injective J]` via `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`.
102	2. `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` — the hard part. Build `I_B` degreewise as
103	   `I_A.cocomplex.X n ⊞ I_C.cocomplex.X n` (biproduct ⇒ injective) with the
104	   **twisted** differential `[[d_A, τ], [0, d_C]]`; the off-diagonal `τ` and the
105	   augmentation `B → I_B^0` are produced stage-by-stage by the injective lifting
106	   property (`Injective.factorThru` against the relevant monos). Model the recursion
107	   on the existing `InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex` / `exact_f_d` /
108	   `ofCocomplex_exactAt_succ` machinery
109	   (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/Injective/Resolution.lean:270–352`). Prove
110	   degreewise-split SES of complexes `0 → I_A → I_B → I_C → 0` and that `I_B`
111	   resolves `B` (exactness via the complex homology LES on the split SES).
112	   Expose the output as a `ShortComplex (CochainComplex C ℕ)` that `.ShortExact`.
113	3. `rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` — apply `G` to the degreewise-split SES (additive
114	   `G` preserves split SES: use `ShortComplex.Splitting.map` /
115	   `Functor.mapShortComplex` on the per-degree splitting), get a `ShortExact` of
116	   `CochainComplex D ℕ`, feed `HomologySequence.δ` + `homology_exact₁/₂/₃`, transport
117	   each `Hⁿ(G(I_•))` to `(Rⁿ G)(•)` via `isoRightDerivedObj`, then kill the acyclic
118	   `(R^{≥1} G)(J)` terms.
119	
120	Feasibility note: the genuinely hard Lean sub-task is the twisted differential `τ`
121	and proving `I_B` is a resolution — a dependent recursion over `ℕ`. This is the
122	dominant risk and should get its own prover objective, scaffolded as a standalone
123	lemma chain (per-degree `τ`, chain-map laws, exactness) rather than one monolithic
124	definition. One cheap simplification to peel off first: the **injective-middle**
125	dimension shift (`0 → A → Q → A' → 0`, `Q` injective) needs NO horseshoe — it is a
126	re-indexing of a single injective resolution of `A` (shift `I_A^{≥1}`) and follows
127	from `isoRightDerivedObj` + `isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`. It does NOT
128	discharge the acyclic-middle case the cosyzygy SESs require, so it cannot replace the
129	horseshoe — but it is a useful warm-up / sanity lemma and may shorten the base degree.
130

Both reports are decisive. Synthesis:

**P4** — route confirmed by the analogist (NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL: the horseshoe is the right, necessary, self-contained gap — both shortcuts are strictly worse). Concrete scaffold shape provided. Gate blocked only by the `lem:homology_long_exact_sequence` anchor must-fix → one writer fix + re-review clears it.

**P3** — the reviewer found a real soundness issue: `lem:cech_acyclic_affine`'s blueprint proof is standard-cover-only but the Lean signature takes a general `X.OpenCover`. This gates the P3 effort-breaker. Resolving it properly (signature refactor + blueprint alignment) is a mini-project competing with the P4 lane.

**Decision: make P4 the single prover lane this iter; defer P3** (resolve its statement gap when P3 becomes active). Run two parallel blueprint-writers (different files): fix the P4 anchor; fix P2 hygiene (push_pull_comp sketch + helper blocks).

Let me read the writer + scaffolder descriptors for directive format.

3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false

10	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
11	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when

13	    multi-route coverage.
14	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
15	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
16	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
17	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond

19	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` markers** (no "after
20	    writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no "verification: confirm

30	    dependency anchor*: a block that states the Mathlib result (in the

32	    declaration, and is marked ``\mathlibok`` so the DAG treats it as
33	    done and the route's reliance on Mathlib stays visible. Name such
34	    anchors in the directive (statement + the Mathlib ``\lean{}``
35	    target). The writer marks ``\mathlibok`` ONLY on these anchors —
36	    never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.

41	      --write-domain 'references/**'

57	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a

59	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
60	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
